data.title: Costco at $1,000: Still a Buy or Time to Wait?
data.description:
Costco Wholesale Corporation (Costco) is a global retail giant that operates on a membership-based warehouse club model. Unlike traditional retailers that focus on high markups, this company acts as a collective buyer for its members, leveraging scale to provide deep discounts on a curated selection of goods. By merging its warehouse operations with its retail storefronts (where the warehouse effectively is the store) it eliminates the expense of separate distribution and storage. The business currently maintains a market cap of roughly $440 billion while operating fewer than 1,000 locations worldwide, generating billions in annual free cash flow while holding a massive cash pile that dwarfs its long-term debt.

It is hard to walk past a warehouse these days without thinking about the $1,000 price tag on the stock. We are sitting at undisputed all-time highs, and the momentum has been relentless, with shares up nearly 12% year-to-date. To put that in perspective, while I have been watching nearby competitors like BJ's Wholesale struggle to keep up with a 7.5% gain, and even the retail giant Walmart trailing at 11%, this company just keeps finding new ways to surprise the market. It wasn't that long ago that even legends like Warren Buffett (Trades, Portfolio) walked away from this position; a move many now calculate left about $1.5 billion on the table. But that is the thing about this business: it has consistently proved the doubters wrong, even when it looks expensive. With a five-year gain of over 200%, the track record is undeniable.

COST Data by GuruFocus

However, a great track record doesn't guarantee a great future return, and as an analyst, I have to ask a much sharper question than "is this a good company?" We all know it's a good company. The real question is: If I am allocating capital today for the next five years, is this elite business still the best place for my dollar, or has the runway already been fully paved into the price? When we frame this against other elite retail businesses, we have to decide if we are buying a growth story that still has legs or if we are merely paying a premium (at a forward multiple approaching 50x for a history lesson.

The Membership Model

When I look at the core of this business, I see something that resembles a high-margin subscription service more than a traditional grocery store. The true value is not found in the markups on the shelves, which are kept notoriously low to maintain member trust, but in the recurring membership fees that provide a steady, predictable stream of income. In the most recent quarter, membership income climbed by 13.6%, driven by a combination of fee increases and a growing base of shoppers. Total paid members have reached 82.1 million, and the renewal rate in the United States and Canada is a staggering 92.1%. This level of retention suggests that once a consumer enters the ecosystem, they find it nearly impossible to leave, viewing the annual fee as a minor entry cost for the significant savings they reap throughout the year.

The depth of this loyalty is best illustrated by the behavior of executive members, who now account for over 75% of total sales. These are the most dedicated customers, and their growth; nearly 10% over the prior year; indicates that the company is successfully upselling its existing base into higher-tier memberships. With 147.2 million total cardholders globally, the reach is massive, yet the model remains incredibly concentrated. The company does not try to be everything to everyone; instead, it focuses on a limited number of stock-keeping units, which allows it to command better pricing from suppliers and pass those savings directly to the consumer. This creates a virtuous cycle where low prices drive traffic, traffic drives membership, and membership provides the capital to keep prices lower than the competition.

However, I have to consider whether this growth can be sustained at the same breakneck pace. While the numbers are currently excellent, there is a generational shift occurring that I find worth watching. Younger consumers are beginning to make up a larger portion of the membership base, and their loyalty patterns are different from previous generations. They are more mobile, more digitally focused, and potentially less tied to a single physical location. While the recent 22% jump in e-commerce sales suggests the company is adapting well to the digital age, the long-term challenge will be maintaining that 92% renewal rate as the demographic makeup of the membership base evolves. If younger members view the warehouse model as less essential than their parents did, the high-margin membership engine could eventually start to lose its torque, even if the current results show no signs of that happening yet.

The Efficiency Gap

If we compare this operation to its largest peer, Walmart, the efficiency gap is nothing short of remarkable. I find it telling that while Walmart has almost 10,000 stores, Costco generates roughly $24.4K of net income per employee, which is more than double the $10.4K generated by Walmart. Even more impressive is the revenue per employee, which stands at $822K compared to Walmart's $340K. This is a fundamental difference in how the business is built. By using the warehouse as the store itself, the company significantly reduces the headcount and real estate costs required to operate. They do not need separate teams to move items from a backroom to a shelf because the backroom is the shelf, and the pallets are the displays. This streamlined approach means that they can move massive volumes with a fraction of the traditional retail overhead, allowing them to scale in ways that their peers simply cannot replicate without a total overhaul of their logistics.

COST Data by GuruFocus

This operational leanness allows for a gross margin that sits right around the 13% mark; a figure that would be a death sentence for most other retailers but works perfectly here because of the membership fee cushion. In the recent second quarter, we saw sales grow by 9.1% to over $68 billion, outperforming the broader retail sector. Comparable sales were also strong, rising 7.4% overall, or 6.7% when adjusting for gas price deflation and foreign exchange. When I look at these figures alongside Walmart's reported comparable sales growth of just 4.6%, it becomes clear that this company is taking market share even in a crowded environment. They are winning because they are more efficient, and that efficiency allows them to be the last to raise prices and the first to lower them as commodity inflation cools. The ability to pass through lower prices on staples like eggs and coffee as inflation eases is a direct result of this efficiency, keeping traffic trends positive even when consumer sentiment is shaky.

We also see this efficiency in how they handle logistical disruptions and macro-economic shifts. For instance, management has been proactive in circumventing supply chain issues and the potential impact of tariffs by moving some production lines domestically. Furthermore, a recent federal court ruling regarding certain global tariffs could result in a windfall of refunds. While the exact amount is uncertain, the company has already indicated that any such windfall would be passed back to members through lower prices and improved value. This commitment to the member-first philosophy is what keeps the traffic coming. During the last quarter, worldwide shopping frequency increased by 3.1%, and the average ticket rose by 4.2%, proving that members are not only coming in more often but are also spending more when they do, even as the broader economy shows signs of fatigue. This resilience is a key differentiator when deciding where to allocate long-term capital.

The Strength of the Balance Sheet

One of the most comforting aspects of this investment case is the sheer health of the balance sheet. With $17.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents against only $5.7 billion in long-term debt, this company certainly qualifies. This lopsided ratio gives management an incredible amount of optionality. They are currently planning to deploy between $5.6 billion and $5.8 billion in capital expenditures, much of which is earmarked for the opening of new warehouses. The plan is to open roughly 28 locations in fiscal 2026 and target more than 30 annual openings starting in fiscal 2027. This steady expansion of the physical footprint provides a clear runway for revenue and membership growth for years to come. In a world where many companies are struggling to find high-return projects, the ability to build and immediately fill 30 warehouses a year is a rare growth lever.

This cash pile also fuels a shareholder-friendly policy that is somewhat unique in the retail space. While the stated dividend yield is relatively low at around 0.5%, the company frequently issues large special dividends when cash levels get high. The last one was a $15 per share payment at the end of 2023. Given that the current payout ratio is only about 24%, I expect another significant increase in the regular dividend or perhaps another special dividend announcement within the year. The company also spent roughly $1.15 billion on dividends in the recent quarter alone, while simultaneously buying back 20 million shares over the past year. This balance between returning cash and reinvesting in expansion is why investors view the company as a "fortress" in turbulent times, making it a natural candidate when searching for an "undisputed great business" to hold.

However, I must acknowledge the risk that this massive cash position could eventually lead to less disciplined spending or over-expansion. While the current plan to open 30 stores a year seems measured, the pressure to maintain high growth rates could lead to expansion into less profitable markets or overpriced acquisitions. Moreover, while the debt levels are low, the company still has to navigate a world of rising interest rates and potential labor market softening. If unemployment ticks up significantly, even the most loyal members might start to reconsider their discretionary spending on high-ticket items. We are already seeing some signs of spending fatigue in the U.S., where comparable sales grew only by a couple of percentage points when excluding gas and digital. The company's resilience is being tested, and while it is passing for now, the margin for error is shrinking as the valuation stays elevated.

Longer-term Patterns and Risks

When I zoom out and look at the longer-term trajectory, the stock has gained over 200% in the last five years, significantly outperforming its peers and the broader market. This is the result of a consistent ability to exceed expectations and adapt to changing consumer tastes. A perfect example of this adaptability was the decision to start selling gold bars. While it seemed like a niche move to some, it turned out to be a massive success, with prices for those bars jumping 150% in value since their introduction. This move attracted new customers and drove significant traffic to the website and warehouses, proving that the company knows exactly how to capture the cultural zeitgeist and turn it into revenue. It shows a management team that isn't just "boring" but is strategically nimble enough to find growth in unconventional corners.

Another long-term pattern that deserves attention is the company's commitment to value leadership in staple items. We have seen them lower prices on essential goods like eggs, cheese, coffee, and paper goods as commodity costs have come down. This is part of a long-standing philosophy that builds immense trust with the consumer. In an era where many retailers are accused of keeping prices artificially high, this company is doing the opposite. By being the last to raise and first to lower prices, they ensure that the value destination tag remains firmly attached to their brand. This creates a psychological moat that is incredibly difficult for competitors like BJ's or Target to bridge, regardless of their own pricing strategies. The gold bar phenomenon shows they can innovate, but the question is whether they can do it fast enough to justify a multiple that rivals high-growth tech companies.

However, no business is without its long-term risks. The ongoing geopolitical tension, particularly the conflict involving the U.S. and Iran, poses a threat to global trade and could drive up shipping costs or cause further supply chain disruptions. While the company has shown a knack for navigating these waters, a prolonged or escalating conflict could eventually weigh on margins. Furthermore, the very strength of the brand (its predictability and boring consistency) could be its undoing if it fails to innovate for a new generation of shoppers. We see that they are pushing digitally-enabled sales, which were up 22.6% in the last quarter, but the core business still relies on the physical trip to the warehouse. Any sustained weakening in the labor market could cause unemployment to tick up, potentially slowing the membership growth that serves as the bedrock for the entire valuation.

Valuation Thoughts and Guru Activity

Now we come to the most contentious part of the analysis: the valuation. As an analyst, I find it difficult to ignore that the stock is currently trading at a forward P/E multiple of 47x earnings. For a retail company, that is an eye-watering figure. To put it in perspective, Walmart trades at about 40x forward earnings, Target at 14x, and BJ's at 21x. While I agree that this company deserves to trade at a premium due to its superior business model, higher ROE (currently around 30.3%), and massive cash pile, a 47x multiple suggests that a lot of future perfection is already priced in. We are looking at a Price/Sales ratio of 1.6x, which is almost triple the 0.6x ratio of BJ's Wholesale. This makes the stock richly priced by almost any traditional metric.

COST Data by GuruFocus

The share price has recently hovered around the $1,000 mark, which creates a psychological barrier for many individual investors. In fact, the last time the stock split was 26 years ago, when they did a 2-for-1 split. Given that peers like Walmart and NVIDIA have all seen massive price gains lead to splits recently, I believe a stock split for this company is not just a possibility, but a necessity to improve accessibility. A 10-for-1 or even a 5-for-1 split would bring the price down to a more manageable level and potentially spark a new wave of retail interest. However, a split does not change the underlying valuation. At almost 50x earnings, the stock is still very expensive, and even with expected earnings growth of 10%, it would take years for the fundamentals to catch up to the current price if the multiple contracts.

If I am allocating capital today for a 5-year horizon, I have to compare this to other elite options. Walmart is cheaper and has its own massive momentum, but its ROE and revenue per employee lag significantly. BJ's and Target offer much more "value" in terms of multiples, but they lack the global expansion runway and the absolute safe haven status that this company commands. The real question is whether you want to pay for the runway that this company still has. Unlike more mature dividend aristocrats that rely solely on pricing power and capital efficiency, this company still has a physical expansion lever to pull, with hundreds of more warehouses potentially to be built globally. With only 942 warehouses expected by the end of 2026, there is still a massive world to conquer. But at a 5x PEG ratio, you are paying for every inch of that runway upfront.

It is always useful to see how the guru investors are positioning themselves in a stock like this. Looking at the most recent filings, the picture is somewhat mixed but generally leans toward a hold or slight reduction for the biggest players. Ken Fisher (Trades, Portfolio) remains the largest individual guru holder with over 3.1 million shares, though he only added a marginal 0.99% to his position recently. This suggests that while he still believes in the long-term story, he is not rushing to buy more at these elevated prices. On the other hand, Renaissance Technologies (Trades, Portfolio) made a massive move, increasing its position by over 5,220%, bringing its total to 693,002 shares. This kind of aggressive buying from a quant-heavy firm suggests they see a momentum or technical tailwind that might not be obvious from the fundamentals alone.

However, other institutional funds are heading for the exits or trimming significantly. The Harbor Capital Appreciation Fund (Trades, Portfolio) reduced its holding by 11%, and Jefferies Group (Trades, Portfolio) slashed its position by a massive 86.97%. Ray Dalio (Trades, Portfolio) and Mario Gabelli (Trades, Portfolio) also reduced their stakes by 26% and 22% respectively. This trend of selling into strength by seasoned value and macro investors is a red flag for me. It indicates that those who have made significant gains over the last five years are starting to take chips off the table, likely concerned about the 50x P/E multiple and the potential for a market correction. When the gurus start thinning their ranks, it is usually a sign that the easy money has already been made. They aren't selling out entirely, but they certainly aren't chasing the $1,000 price tag with fresh capital.

There are a few new entrants, like Steven Cohen (Trades, Portfolio) and Lee Ainslie (Trades, Portfolio), who have initiated small new holdings, but these are relatively minor in the grand scheme of the shares outstanding. The fact that the total number of investors in this elite group is small suggests that this is a highly concentrated bet. If a few more of these gurus decide that the valuation has finally disconnected from reality, we could see a wave of institutional selling that puts downward pressure on the stock. For now, the hold sentiment seems to be the consensus among the big players, with most content to sit on their existing piles and wait for a better entry point or a clearer signal of the next growth phase. If the smartest capital in the room is standing pat or trimming, we should be very cautious about being the ones to jump in at the top.

Final Thoughts

After weighing all the evidence, I find myself in a bit of a quandary. On one hand, this is arguably one of the best-run businesses in the world. Its membership model is a work of genius, its balance sheet is a fortress, and its operational efficiency makes its competitors look sluggish by comparison. The company has shown it can thrive in inflationary environments and navigate supply chain crises. If I were looking for a stock to hold for the next twenty years and never look at the price, this would be at the top of my list. The fundamentals are rock solid, and the long-term runway for expansion is clear.

On the other hand, as an analyst looking at the here and now, I cannot ignore that the stock is currently trading at a 50x forward P/E multiple. When I look at the capital allocation choice, I see a business that is too good to sell but too expensive to buy. Compared to Walmart or BJ's, you are getting a significantly better operation, but you are paying a massive premium for it that likely caps your 5-year upside to the mid-to-high single digits. The valuation has reached a point where it is priced for perfection, leaving almost no room for the inevitable macro-economic hiccups or competitive pressures that will eventually arise. We are seeing some signs of cooling in U.S. consumer spending, and while the company is currently outperforming its peers, it is not immune to a broader economic downturn. The risk of a significant price pullback in a market correction is high.

My final recommendation is a Hold. For those who already own the stock, there is no reason to sell a winner of this caliber, especially with the potential for a special dividend or a stock split on the horizon. However, for prospective investors looking to initiate a new position, the current price is simply too high for comfort. If you are allocating new capital today, you are likely to find better risk-adjusted returns elsewhere in the retail sector or in other "elite" businesses that aren't trading at all-time highs with a 5.0 PEG ratio. I believe the prudent move is to wait for a more attractive entry point; perhaps a dip back toward the $850 or $900 level; before putting new capital to work. This remains a five-star business currently trading at a six-star price.

data.title: ONEOK: Midstream's Best Risk-Adjusted Value Play
data.description:
Thesis

ONEOK has doubled its EBITDA in just two years and the market hasn't caught up. At approximately $87 per share, OKE trades at a roughly 20% discount to its five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple, offering investors a rare combination: a nearly 5% dividend yield growing 34% annually, a fee-based business model generating $8 billion in adjusted EBITDA, and a multi-billion-dollar growth backlog set to unlock significant free cash flow starting in 2027.

The stock's selloff driven by conservative 2026 guidance reflecting $5560 WTI assumptions has created a compelling entry point for a fundamentally transformed company. ONEOK today operates the longest refined products pipeline system in America, processes over 5.5 Bcf/d of natural gas, and has captured $475 million in acquisition synergies that already exceed original targets by more than 2x. At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, the risk-reward skews decisively bullish.

The Defining KPI: Adjusted EBITDA Growth

The single most compelling metric for the OKE bull case is adjusted EBITDA growth. ONEOK has delivered 12 consecutive years of EBITDA growth from 2013 through 2025, compounding at a greater than 16% annualized rate. From the COVID trough of $2.7 billion in 2020, EBITDA has tripled to $8.0 billion a five-year CAGR of approximately 24%. No other large-cap midstream company has matched this trajectory.

The 2026 guidance of $8.1 billion at midpoint essentially flat year-over-year represents what management has framed as a consolidation year built on conservative $5560 WTI assumptions. If WTI averages closer to $65 than the guided $57.50 midpoint, EBITDA could exceed $8.5 billion and resume the growth trajectory. The EBITDA bridge shows roughly $335 million in positive contributions from volume growth, synergies, and project completions, offset by $375 million in headwinds from lower commodity price assumptions.

Financial Summary

Synergy Capture: Ahead of Schedule and Accelerating

The strongest near-term bullish signal is ONEOK's synergy performance. When the $18.8 billion Magellan acquisition was announced, management projected at least $200 million in annual base synergies. The results have blown past those targets: through year-end 2025, ONEOK achieved $475 million in cumulative EBITDA synergies more than double the original base target. Including non-cash compensation and supply chain savings, total life-to-date identified synergies reached $707 million.

Management has guided for an additional $150 million in incremental synergies in 2026, bringing the cumulative EBITDA total to approximately $625 million by year-end 2026, with a stated goal of exceeding $700 million. The synergy sources are diversified: commercial and operations synergies contributed $321 million, asset optimization added $175 million, batching and blending efficiencies generated $143 million, and corporate cost reductions delivered $154 million. Market share gains, still in early innings at just $3 million to date, are expected to add $34 million in 2026 alone suggesting the commercial synergy runway has significant upside remaining.

An additional and often overlooked benefit: the Magellan deal created a tax basis step-up worth an estimated $3.0 billion (NPV of approximately $1.5 billion), which defers meaningful cash tax payments until 2029. This effectively increases free cash flow available for debt reduction and shareholder returns throughout the integration period.

Growth Drivers ? P&L Bridge

ONEOK's current capex cycle represents its most ambitious organic growth buildout in company history, with total capital expenditures of $3.15 billion in 2025 and guidance of $2.7$3.2 billion for 2026. Each growth vector feeds directly into the income statement:

Management projects historical EBITDA multiples of 46x on organic growth capital. At the guided approximately $2.4 billion in 2026 growth capex, this implies $400600 million in incremental EBITDA as projects reach full contribution the bulk of which arrives in 20272028. This is the inflection the market is underpricing.

Peer Comparison: Discounted Despite Superior Growth Track Record

At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, OKE trades at a meaningful discount to Williams Companies (12.4x), Kinder Morgan (14.5x), and Targa Resources (12.5x) despite having delivered the highest EBITDA growth in the peer group over the past three years. Only Enterprise Products Partners (9.5x) and Energy Transfer (5.8x, reflecting its MLP structure) trade cheaper. The discount reflects market concerns about leverage and the pause in EBITDA growth, but both are temporary conditions that should resolve as capex normalizes and projects contribute.

Valuation Sanity Check

At approximately $86.93, OKE trades at 10.8x NTM EV/EBITDA and 15.5x NTM earnings. Applying a 12x multiple a modest premium reflecting successful integration, still below the historical 1314x average to the top end of 2026 EBITDA guidance ($8.3 billion) yields an enterprise value of $99.6 billion. After subtracting approximately $33 billion in net debt and dividing by 630 million shares, this implies a fair value of approximately $106 per share roughly 22% upside from current levels. Add the 4.9% dividend yield, and total return potential exceeds 25%.

The annualized dividend of $4.28 carries 56 consecutive years of payments. Management's stated 34% annual growth target, combined with a $2 billion share repurchase authorization, provides a clear capital return framework. The forward P/E of 15.5x against expected EPS of $5.45 further underscores the value relative to peers like Williams (24x) and Kinder Morgan (24.4x).

Risks

The bear case centers on three interrelated concerns. First, leverage at 3.8x Debt/EBITDA is the highest among major C-corp midstream peers and leaves limited margin for EBITDA erosion the interest coverage ratio of 3.3x is adequate but not robust. However, ONEOK retired $3.1 billion in debt in 2025 alone, and the absence of cash taxes until 2029 supports a clear deleveraging glide path.

Second, the flat 2026 EBITDA guidance has raised questions about whether the growth streak is ending rather than pausing. This reflects management's conservative $5560 WTI assumptions at prices just $510 above the guided range, EBITDA would exceed $8.3 billion and resume the growth trajectory.

Third, Permian Basin drilling headwinds could pressure volumes if crude prices remain in the mid-$50s, with rig counts already down roughly 33% from late 2022 peaks. However, structural production growth from rising gas-to-oil ratios means NGL and gas volumes can grow even with fewer rigs operating. The integration complexity of absorbing three major acquisitions simultaneously ($25B+ in deal value) is arguably the most material risk, yet the synergy performance to date validates management's execution capability.

Conclusion

ONEOK represents a rare combination of value, yield, and growth in today's midstream sector. The company has executed one of the most ambitious transformation strategies in energy infrastructure tripling EBITDA in five years while shifting to a 90% fee-based model and is now being penalized for a single year of conservative guidance built on bearish commodity assumptions.At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, a 4.9% yield, and a clear path to accelerating free cash flow generation in 2027, the risk-reward skews decisively bullish. The $475 million in synergies already captured validates the acquisition strategy, the $5 billion growth backlog provides multi-year earnings visibility, and the approaching capex inflection should catalyze both deleveraging and enhanced shareholder returns. For investors willing to look past a transitory guidance disappointment, OKE at $87 offers roughly 22% upside to fair value plus a growing nearly 5% dividend making it the most compelling risk-adjusted opportunity in large-cap midstream today.
